nativeRange.habitat: The African Arowana inhabits slow-moving or still water across its range, including large rivers, floodplains, swamps, lakes, and marshes with abundant aquatic vegetation. It favours shallow, warm, and turbid water rich in plankton and organic matter.

decorPreferences: robust emergent or floating plants (water hyacinth, Amazon frogbit)
smooth large boulders or driftwood as cover

diet.notes: The African Arowana is highly unusual among arowanas in being a filter-feeder and planktivore rather than a fish-eating predator. It possesses a specialised epibranchial organ (a spiral-shaped structure above the gill arches) that strains zooplankton, phytoplankton, algae, and fine organic particles from the water column — functioning much as the gill rakers of filter-feeding sardines and herrings do. Feeding in aquaria is challenging. Finely ground spirulina powder, liquid phytoplankton, copepods, daphnia, very fine zooplankton suspensions, and high-quality micro-pellets no larger than 1 mm can be attempted. Live or cultured rotifers and brine shrimp nauplii are often accepted. The species will also take small live invertebrates opportunistically, but the large meaty prey and feeder fish typical for other arowana species should be avoided.

goodWith: large robust catfish (Synodontis spp.) in very large tanks
large cichlids of similar size if space allows
avoidWith: small fish (will be eaten or harassed)
any fish small enough to be swallowed

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Petrtyl; 1920x1280. Sharp lateral profile of a live individual in an aquarium, whole fish in frame, showing the elongated bonytongue body, large scales and olive-silver coloration.
Photo by Brent Tibbatts; 600x337. Live specimen in a public-aquarium display, whole fish shown; lower resolution so kept only as a secondary image.

summary: The African Arowana (Heterotis niloticus) is a large, air-breathing bonytongue from West and Central Africa that can reach 100 cm in length. Unlike all other arowanas in the hobby, it is a filter-feeder rather than a predator, using a specialised internal organ to strain plankton from the water. Adults outgrow all home aquariums.
careNotes: The African Arowana is the sole member of the genus Heterotis and is distinct from Asian (Scleropages) and South American (Osteoglossum) arowanas in both appearance and feeding biology. It is a facultative air-breather, meaning it surfaces regularly to gulp atmospheric oxygen via a modified swim bladder. Tanks must provide open water surface access and a securely weighted lid, as this species is a powerful jumper. Adults routinely reach 90–100 cm in total length, making the African Arowana wholly unsuitable for home aquaria at any stage beyond early juvenilhood. Juveniles are occasionally exported for the aquarium trade, typically at 5–15 cm, but they grow extremely rapidly — several centimetres per month under good conditions — and will require an institutional-scale system of 2,000 litres or more within two to three years. Water quality demands are moderate: the species tolerates soft to moderately hard water with a pH of 6.5–7.5 and a general hardness of 4–15 dGH, at tropical temperatures of 24–30 °C. The primary challenge in captivity is diet. Unlike all other aquarium arowanas, the African Arowana will not reliably accept standard arowana foods such as large pellets, feeder fish, or meaty prey. Its specialised epibranchial organ is adapted for filtering plankton, so the diet should centre on spirulina suspensions, live and frozen zooplankton, daphnia, copepods, rotifers, and very small pellets no larger than 1 mm. The species is of major importance in West African aquaculture as a fast-growing food fish and is farmed extensively in ponds across Senegal, Guinea, and elsewhere; wild populations are protected by local fisheries regulations in some range states. The African Arowana is not related to the Asian Arowana (Scleropages formosus) and is not subject to the CITES Appendix I listing that restricts trade in that species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is unreported in home aquaria and extremely rare in institutional settings. In the wild, pairs construct large circular nests of aquatic vegetation in shallow flooded areas; both parents guard the eggs and early fry. Fry are initially planktonic feeders. The species reaches sexual maturity at approximately 40–50 cm. Successful captive reproduction has been documented in large aquaculture pond systems in West Africa, but replication in enclosed aquaria is not considered feasible.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Heterotis niloticus
primary.notes: Primary source. Species profile covering adult size (to 100 cm), water parameters, diet (planktivore/filter-feeder, epibranchial organ), native range (West and Central Africa), temperament, tank requirements, and breeding notes. Confirmed planktivore diet type and the unusual epibranchial filter organ distinguishing this species from all other arowanas.

additional.name: FishBase — Heterotis niloticus
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (family Arapaimidae, order Osteoglossiformes), adult size (max 100 cm TL), native range (Nile, Niger, Senegal, Gambia, Volta, Chad basins and coastal West African rivers), temperature range (22–30°C), diet category (planktivore), and aquaculture importance. Confirmed facultative air-breather and food-fish status in West Africa.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Arowana species profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented profile confirming the species’ unsuitability for home aquaria, filter-feeding diet, and rapid growth rate. Noted the species is occasionally traded as juveniles but reaches unmanageable size quickly. Supported the expert care level and 2000 L advisory.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Arowana (Heterotis niloticus)
additional.notes: Referenced for hobbyist trade perspective and diet guidance. Confirmed that the species requires a specialised plankton-based diet unlike other arowanas and is not recommended for home aquarists due to adult size.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Heterotis niloticus care and aquaculture
additional.notes: Supplementary reference covering aquaculture use in West Africa, epibranchial organ function, and captive feeding strategies. Confirmed the species’ distinction from Asian/South American arowanas and noted successful pond aquaculture in Senegal and Guinea.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been achieved in home aquaria. In the wild, pairs construct circular vegetation nests in shallow flooded habitats and guard both eggs and early fry. Breeding at aquaculture scale in outdoor pond systems has been documented in West Africa. The species is thought to reach sexual maturity at approximately 40–50 cm in total length.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches ~100cm; food fish in West African aquaculture; needs 2000L+ tanks. Sold as small juveniles but rapidly outgrows all home setups.
